segments.title: HMS Tiger 15-inch upgrade counterfactual starts from the real ship constraints
segments.summary: The episode explicitly splits historical setup from alternate history before moving into HMS Tiger gun and barbette constraints.
segments.body: The later scenario is meant to rest on real history and ship-design constraints.

evidence.note: Opening explains the history-first structure and introduces the 13.5-inch barbette issue.

segments.title: Tiger barbette feasibility for 15-inch guns
segments.summary: The technical opening compares 13.5-inch and 15-inch barbette requirements and Tiger hull dimensions.
segments.body: The opening grounds the Tiger counterfactual in physical engineering rather than wishful refitting. Dr. Clarke compares the 13.5-inch and 15-inch barbette requirements against the Tiger hull to ask whether the larger gun can fit at all. That makes the later strategic argument depend on ship dimensions, not only on a preference for heavier weapons.

evidence.note: The technical opening compares 13.5-inch and 15-inch barbette requirements and Tiger hull dimensions.

segments.title: Washington Naval Treaty viability for upgrading Tiger
segments.summary: The treaty section asks whether an upgraded Tiger would exceed capital-ship limits.
segments.body: This partitions the legal/treaty argument from the physical refit argument.

evidence.note: The treaty section asks whether an upgraded Tiger would exceed capital-ship limits.

segments.title: Hull density and armour support
segments.summary: The transcript explains why denser internals and stronger subdivision matter when adding armour and heavy changes.
segments.body: The discussion captures the naval-architecture logic behind possible modernization.

evidence.note: The transcript explains why denser internals and stronger subdivision matter when adding armour and heavy changes.

segments.title: Courageous-class turrets create an upgrade opportunity
segments.summary: The episode notes that 15-inch guns and turrets from Courageous-class ships could be available.
segments.body: The discussion marks the material path that makes the alternate history easier.

evidence.note: The episode notes that 15-inch guns and turrets from Courageous-class ships could be available.

segments.title: Frederick Field and strategic speed logic
segments.summary: The scenario develops around Frederick Field, speed, and what the Royal Navy needs fast capital ships to do.
segments.body: The discussion separates operational rationale from pure gun replacement.

evidence.note: The scenario develops around Frederick Field, speed, and what the Royal Navy needs fast capital ships to do.

segments.title: 15-inch standardisation instead of 16-inch escalation
segments.summary: The answer explores whether Britain might standardize on 15-inch guns rather than chase 16-inch guns.
segments.body: The answer moves from Tiger herself to British capital-ship standardization. Dr. Clarke asks whether the Royal Navy might have consolidated around the 15-inch gun instead of chasing a 16-inch escalation path. The point is useful because it links a single battlecruiser design choice to wider fleet planning and industrial continuity.

evidence.note: The answer explores whether Britain might standardize on 15-inch guns rather than chase 16-inch guns.

segments.title: Fleet effects of a rebuilt Tiger
segments.summary: The later section considers Tiger alongside Hood, Renown, and Repulse as a fast 15-inch capital-ship group.
segments.body: The later section tests the rebuilt Tiger against a fast capital-ship force that already includes Hood, Renown, and Repulse. Dr. Clarke uses the grouping to ask whether another 15-inch fast ship changes deployment choices, deterrence, and the balance between legacy battlecruisers and newer capital ships. The counterfactual payoff is fleet composition, not just a larger gun fit.

evidence.note: The later section considers Tiger alongside Hood, Renown, and Repulse as a fast 15-inch capital-ship group.
